L2 Smaller values more similar [0, ∞) Euclidean distance
IP Larger values more similar [-1, 1] Inner product
COSINE Larger values more similar [-1, 1] Cosine similarity
JACCARD Smaller values more similar [0, 1] Jaccard distance
MHJACCARD Smaller values more similar [0, 1] MinHash Jaccard estimate
HAMMING Smaller values more similar [0, dim(vector)] Hamming distance
BM25 Higher scores more relevant [0, ∞) Text relevance scoring

1. Euclidean Distance (L2)

Definition: Measures straight-line distance between two points.

Formula:

d(a, b) = √(Σ(ai - bi)²)

Where a = (a0, a1, …, an-1) and b = (b0, b1, …, bn-1) are two points in n-dimensional space.

Characteristics:

  • Most commonly used distance metric
  • Suitable for continuous data
  • Milvus doesn’t calculate square root in actual computation (performance boost)

Applicable scenarios:

  • Image similarity search (face recognition)
  • General vector search
  • Scenarios with uniform data distribution

Real examples:

  • Two face photos’ feature vectors, smaller L2 distance means more similar people
  • Product feature vectors, smaller L2 distance means closer attributes

2. Inner Product (IP)

Definition: Inner product value of two vectors.

Formula:

IP(a, b) = Σ(ai × bi)

Characteristics:

  • Larger values indicate more similarity
  • Considers both magnitude and angle of vectors
  • Suitable for non-normalized data

Important note:

  • If using IP to calculate similarity, must normalize vectors first
  • After normalization, IP equals cosine similarity

Applicable scenarios:

  • Recommendation systems (user-item rating prediction)
  • Scenarios where vector magnitude matters
  • Already normalized vector data

Real examples:

  • Movie rating prediction, larger IP values mean user likes the movie more
  • Document relevance ranking

3. Cosine Similarity

Definition: Uses cosine of angle between two vectors to measure similarity.

Formula:

cosine(a, b) = (Σ(ai × bi)) / (√(Σai²) × √(Σbi²))

Characteristics:

  • Value range [-1, 1]
  • Focuses only on direction, not magnitude
  • 1 means identical direction, 0 means orthogonal, -1 means completely opposite

Applicable scenarios (most common):

  • Text similarity (NLP)
  • Semantic search
  • Recommendation systems
  • Any scenario ignoring vector magnitude

Real examples:

  • Two articles’ semantic similarity, larger COSINE values mean closer topics
  • User interest vector matching, high COSINE values mean similar interests
  • OpenAI/Cohere models’ embedding defaults to COSINE

4. Jaccard Distance

Definition: Measures similarity between two sets, equal to intersection size divided by union size.

Formula:

JACCARD similarity = |A ∩ B| / |A ∪ B|
JACCARD distance = 1 - JACCARD similarity

Characteristics:

  • Only suitable for finite sample sets
  • Value range [0, 1]
  • For binary variables, equivalent to Tanimoto coefficient

Applicable scenarios:

  • Binary vectors (BINARY_VECTOR)
  • Set similarity comparison
  • Label matching

Real examples:

  • User label matching: User A labels {tech, music, travel}, User B labels {tech, movie, travel}, intersection 2, union 4, JACCARD similarity = 2/4 = 0.5
  • Document keyword overlap

5. MinHash Jaccard (MHJACCARD)

Definition: Method using MinHash signatures to quickly estimate Jaccard similarity.

Characteristics:

  • Much faster than exact JACCARD calculation
  • Suitable for large-scale or high-dimensional scenarios
  • Distance = 1 - estimated similarity

Applicable scenarios:

  • Large document collection similarity
  • Large-scale user group similarity analysis
  • Genome k-mer set comparison

Real examples:

  • Massive webpage deduplication
  • Large-scale user population similarity analysis

6. Hamming Distance

Definition: Number of different bits in two equal-length binary strings.

Formula:

Example: 11011001 ⊕ 10011101 = 01000100
Contains 2 ones, so HAMMING distance = 2

Characteristics:

  • Only suitable for binary data
  • Value range [0, dim(vector)]
  • Extremely fast computation

Applicable scenarios:

  • Binary feature vectors (BINARY_VECTOR)
  • Image hash matching
  • Error detection and correction

Real examples:

  • Image perceptual hash (pHash) similarity comparison
  • Binary fingerprint matching

7. BM25 Similarity

Definition: Text relevance scoring method specifically designed for full-text search.

Core factors:

  1. Term Frequency (TF): Frequency of term in document
  2. Inverse Document Frequency (IDF): Term importance across corpus
  3. Document length normalization: Avoid long documents getting higher scores

Formula:

score(D, Q) = Σ IDF(qi) × [TF(qi, D) × (k1 + 1)] / [TF(qi, D) + k1 × (1 - b + b × |D|/avgdl)]

Parameter explanation:

  • k1: Controls TF influence, typical range [1.2, 2.0], Milvus allows [0, 3]
  • b: Controls length normalization degree, range [0, 1]
    • b=0: No normalization
    • b=1: Full normalization
  • avgdl: Average document length in corpus

Applicable scenarios:

  • Full-text search (SPARSE_FLOAT_VECTOR)
  • Document retrieval
  • Question-answering systems

Real examples:

  • Search engine keyword matching
  • Knowledge base QA relevance ranking

How to Choose Metric Types?

Application Scenario Recommended Metric Type Reason
Semantic search, NLP COSINE Only focuses on direction, unaffected by vector magnitude
Image search, face recognition L2 Intuitive distance metric, suitable for continuous features
Recommendation systems (normalized vectors) COSINE or IP Equivalent after normalization
Recommendation systems (non-normalized) IP Considers rating magnitude
Binary feature matching HAMMING Fast speed, suitable for binary data
Set similarity JACCARD Suitable for labels, keyword matching
Full-text search BM25 Specifically designed for text retrieval
Large-scale set matching MHJACCARD Fast estimation, suitable for massive data

Usage Examples 

# Vector field using different metric types
index_params.add_index(
 field_name="embedding",
 index_type="HNSW",
 metric_type="COSINE" # Recommended for semantic search
)

index_params.add_index(
 field_name="image_vector",
 index_type="IVF_FLAT",
 metric_type="L2" # Recommended for image search
)

index_params.add_index(
 field_name="binary_features",
 index_type="BIN_FLAT",
 metric_type="HAMMING" # Recommended for binary features
)

index_params.add_index(
 field_name="sparse_vector",
 index_type="SPARSE_INVERTED_INDEX",
 metric_type="BM25" # Recommended for full-text search
)

Performance Comparison

Metric Type Calculation Speed Memory Usage Precision Applicable Data Types
HAMMING Extremely fast Very low Exact Binary
L2 Fast Medium Exact Float
IP Fast Medium Exact Float
COSINE Medium Medium Exact Float
JACCARD Medium Low Exact Binary/Sets
MHJACCARD Fast Low Approximate Binary/Sets
BM25 Medium Medium Exact Sparse vectors

Summary

Milvus vector indexes adopt a layered architecture (coarse filtering → quantization compression → refinement for precision improvement), adaptively optimizing the trade-off between accuracy and performance. For practical selection, recommend combining data scale, hardware environment (memory/disk/GPU), query patterns (topK size, filter ratio) and business recall rate requirements for experimental tuning rather than one-size-fits-all approach.

Key decision points:

  1. Choose index type: Based on capacity, recall rate, filter ratio (HNSW/IVF/DiskANN/FLAT)
  2. Choose metric type: Based on data characteristics and application scenarios (COSINE/L2/IP/HAMMING/BM25)
  3. Tune parameters: Fine-tune index parameters and search parameters based on actual test results

item.title: Milvus Collection Operations Detailed Guide
item.description:
1. What is Collection

1.1 Concept Understanding

Collection is the core concept in Milvus, similar to a table in relational databases. Collections are used to store vector data and metadata with the same structure.

Milvus Concept Relational Database Concept Description
Collection Table Container for structured data
Field Column Data attribute fields
Entity Row A complete data record
Schema Table Schema Defines data structure and constraints
Vector Field - Milvus-specific vector field

1.2 Characteristics of Collections

  • Schema Definition: Collections must have a clear Schema defining all field types and attributes
  • Vector Storage: Must contain at least one vector field (dense or sparse vectors)
  • Index Support: Supports vector indexes and scalar indexes to accelerate queries
  • Sharding Mechanism: Supports horizontal sharding to improve concurrent processing capability

2. Schema Design

2.1 Helper Function for Creating Schema 

from pymilvus import MilvusClient, DataType, CollectionSchema

def initialize_schema(field_definitions: list[dict]) -> CollectionSchema:
 """
 Batch create Schema

 Args:
 field_definitions: List of field definitions, each element is a field configuration dictionary

 Returns:
 CollectionSchema: Created Schema object
 """
 schema_obj = client.create_schema()
 for field_config in field_definitions:
 schema_obj.add_field(**field_config)
 return schema_obj

2.2 Supported Data Types

2.2.1 Primary Key Types

  • INT64: 64-bit integer, supports auto-increment
  • VARCHAR: String type, requires max_length

2.2.2 Scalar Types

  • BOOL: Boolean values
  • INT8, INT16, INT32, INT64: Integer types
  • FLOAT, DOUBLE: Floating-point types
  • VARCHAR: String type

2.2.3 Complex Types

  • JSON: Store JSON format data
  • ARRAY: Array type, requires element_type and max_capacity
  • STRUCT: Struct type, for nested data
  • TIMESTAMPTZ: Timestamp with timezone
  • GEOMETRY: Geometry type (e.g., coordinates)

2.2.4 Vector Types

  • FLOAT_VECTOR: Dense floating-point vectors (most commonly used)
  • FLOAT16_VECTOR: Half-precision floating-point vectors
  • BFLOAT16_VECTOR: Brain Float16 vectors
  • INT8_VECTOR: 8-bit integer vectors
  • BINARY_VECTOR: Binary vectors
  • SPARSE_FLOAT_VECTOR: Sparse floating-point vectors

2.3 Practical Example: Intelligent Library Management System 

from pymilvus import MilvusClient, DataType

# Define Schema
schemas_list = [
 {
 "field_name": "book_id",
 "datatype": DataType.INT64,
 "is_primary": True,
 "auto_id": True, # Auto-increment primary key
 "mmap_enabled": True, # Enable memory mapping
 "description": "Book ID, auto-increment"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "title",
 "datatype": DataType.VARCHAR,
 "max_length": 500,
 "description": "Book title"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "isbn",
 "datatype": DataType.VARCHAR,
 "max_length": 20,
 "description": "Book ISBN number"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "is_available",
 "datatype": DataType.BOOL,
 "description": "Whether available in library"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "page_count",
 "datatype": DataType.INT32,
 "description": "Page count"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "rating",
 "datatype": DataType.FLOAT,
 "description": "Rating"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "price",
 "datatype": DataType.DOUBLE,
 "description": "Price"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "details",
 "datatype": DataType.JSON,
 "description": "Book details including author, publisher, etc."
 },
 {
 "field_name": "tags",
 "datatype": DataType.ARRAY,
 "element_type": DataType.VARCHAR,
 "max_length": 255, # Maximum length of array elements
 "max_capacity": 20, # Maximum array capacity
 "description": "Tag list"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "borrow_history",
 "datatype": DataType.ARRAY,
 "element_type": DataType.STRUCT,
 "max_capacity": 2000,
 "struct_schema": initialize_struct_field([
 {
 "field_name": "borrower_id",
 "datatype": DataType.INT64,
 "description": "Borrower ID"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "borrower_name",
 "datatype": DataType.VARCHAR,
 "max_length": 50,
 "description": "Borrower name"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "borrow_time",
 "datatype": DataType.VARCHAR,
 "max_length": 100,
 "description": "Borrow time"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "return_time",
 "datatype": DataType.VARCHAR,
 "max_length": 100,
 "description": "Return time"
 }
 ]),
 "description": "Borrowing history records"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "publish_date",
 "datatype": DataType.TIMESTAMPTZ,
 "description": "Publication date"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "added_time",
 "datatype": DataType.TIMESTAMPTZ,
 "description": "Inventory time"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "location",
 "datatype": DataType.GEOMETRY,
 "description": "Book location"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "content_vector",
 "datatype": DataType.FLOAT_VECTOR,
 "dim": 256, # Vector dimension
 "description": "Content feature vector, dense vector"
 },
 {
 "field_name": "keyword_vector",
 "datatype": DataType.SPARSE_FLOAT_VECTOR,
 "description": "Sparse vector - keyword vector"
 }
]

# Create Schema
schemas = initialize_schema(schemas_list)

2.4 Creating StructField

For nested struct data, use StructFieldSchema:

def initialize_struct_field(field_definitions: list[dict]) -> StructFieldSchema:
 """
 Create StructFieldSchema object

 Args:
 field_definitions: List of field definitions

 Returns:
 StructFieldSchema: Created StructFieldSchema object
 """
 struct_field_obj = client.create_struct_field_schema()
 for field_config in field_definitions:
 struct_field_obj.add_field(**field_config)
 return struct_field_obj

3. Index Configuration

Before creating collections, configure indexes for vector fields and primary key fields.

# Prepare index parameters
index_params = client.prepare_index_params()

# Primary key index
index_params.add_index(
 field_name="book_id",
 index_type="AUTOINDEX"
)

# Dense vector index
index_params.add_index(
 field_name="content_vector",
 index_type="AUTOINDEX",
 metric_type="COSINE" # Cosine similarity
)

# Sparse vector index
index_params.add_index(
 field_name="keyword_vector",
 index_type="AUTOINDEX",
 metric_type="IP" # Inner product
)

Metric type explanations:

  • COSINE: Cosine similarity, suitable for text vectors
  • IP: Inner product, suitable for sparse vectors
  • L2: Euclidean distance, suitable for image vectors

4. Basic Collection Operations

4.1 Creating Collections 

collection_name = "book_collection"

# Check if collection exists
if not client.has_collection(collection_name):
 client.create_collection(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 schema=schemas,
 index_params=index_params
 )
 print(f"📚 Collection '{collection_name}' created successfully")

4.1.1 Optional Parameters for Creating Collections

Parameter Description Default Value
collection_name Collection name Required
schema Collection data structure Required
index_params Index parameters Optional
num_shards Number of shards 1
enable_mmap Enable memory mapping True
consistency_level Consistency level Session

4.1.2 Sharding (Shards) Explanation

Shards are horizontal slices of collections, each shard corresponds to a data input channel. Reasonable shard configuration can improve concurrent write performance.

client.create_collection(
 collection_name="large_collection",
 schema=schemas,
 index_params=index_params,
 num_shards=4 # 4 shards, suitable for high-concurrency writes
)

Shard quantity recommendations:

  • Small-scale data (< 1 million): 1-2 shards
  • Medium-scale data (1M-10M): 2-4 shards
  • Large-scale data (> 10M): 4-8 shards

4.1.3 Memory Mapping (mmap)

Memory mapping allows Milvus to map raw field data to memory instead of fully loading it, thus reducing memory usage.

# Enable mmap at field level
{
 "field_name": "book_id",
 "datatype": DataType.INT64,
 "is_primary": True,
 "mmap_enabled": True # Field-level mmap
}

# Enable mmap at collection level
client.create_collection(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 schema=schemas,
 enable_mmap=True # Collection-level mmap
)

4.1.4 Consistency Level

Milvus supports four consistency levels:

Level Description Applicable Scenarios
Strong Strong consistency, read latest written data Financial/transaction systems
Bounded Bounded consistency, consistent within time window Real-time recommendation systems
Session Session consistency, consistent within same session (default) Production environment (recommended)
Eventually Eventual consistency, acceptable temporary inconsistency Big data analysis 
client.create_collection(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 schema=schemas,
 index_params=index_params,
 consistency_level="Session" # Recommended
)

4.1.5 Collection TTL (Time-To-Live)

Set data expiration time for collections; Milvus automatically deletes expired data.

# Set TTL during creation
client.create_collection(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 schema=schemas,
 properties={"collection.ttl.seconds": 86400} # Expire after 24 hours
)

# Or modify TTL after creation
client.alter_collection_properties(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 properties={"collection.ttl.seconds": 60} # Expire after 60 seconds
)

4.2 Viewing Collection List 

# List all collections in current database
collections = client.list_collections()
print(f"📚 Current database collection list: {collections}")

# Compare with MySQL
# SHOW TABLES;

4.3 Checking Collection Existence 

# Check if collection exists
exists = client.has_collection(collection_name)
print(f"📚 Collection '{collection_name}' exists: {exists}")

4.4 Viewing Collection Details 

# Describe collection details
collection_info = client.describe_collection(collection_name)
print(f"📚 Collection '{collection_name}' description: {collection_info}")

Output example:

{
 'collection_name': 'book_collection',
 'auto_id': True,
 'num_shards': 1,
 'description': '',
 'fields': [
 {'field_id': 100, 'name': 'book_id', 'type': 5, 'is_primary': True},
 {'field_id': 101, 'name': 'title', 'type': 21, 'max_length': 500},
 # ... other fields
 ],
 'consistency_level': 'Session'
}

4.5 Renaming Collections 

# Rename collection
new_collection_name = "new_book_collection"
client.rename_collection(
 old_name=collection_name,
 new_name=new_collection_name
)
print(f"📚 Collection '{collection_name}' renamed successfully, new name: {new_collection_name}")

# Compare with MySQL
# RENAME TABLE old_name TO new_name;

4.6 Modifying Collection Properties 

# Set collection properties
client.alter_collection_properties(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 properties={
 "collection.ttl.seconds": 86400, # Set TTL
 "mmap.enabled": True, # Enable mmap
 "partitionkey.isolation": True, # Enable partition key isolation
 "dynamicfield.enabled": True, # Enable dynamic fields
 "allow_insert_auto_id": False, # Don't allow user-provided primary keys
 "timezone": "Asia/Shanghai" # Set timezone
 }
)

4.6.1 Supported Modifiable Properties

Property Description
collection.ttl.seconds Data expiration time (seconds)
mmap.enabled Enable memory mapping
partitionkey.isolation Enable partition key isolation to improve search performance
dynamicfield.enabled Enable dynamic fields, allowing insertion of undefined fields
allow_insert_auto_id Whether to allow users to provide auto-generated primary key values
timezone Collection’s default timezone

4.7 Deleting Collections 

# Delete collection
client.drop_collection(collection_name=collection_name)
print(f"📚 Collection '{collection_name}' deleted")

# Compare with MySQL
# DROP TABLE table_name;

5. Collection Loading and Unloading

5.1 Why Load Collections?

In Milvus, collections are in unloaded state by default after creation. Only after loading into memory can search and query operations be performed. When loading collections, Milvus loads index files and raw data into memory.

5.2 Loading Entire Collection 

# Load collection (must create index first)
client.load_collection(collection_name=collection_name)

# Check load status
res = client.get_load_state(collection_name=collection_name)
print(f"📚 Collection '{collection_name}' load status: {res}")

Load status explanation:

  • LoadStateNotLoad: Not loaded
  • LoadStateLoading: Loading
  • LoadStateLoaded: Loaded

5.3 Loading Specific Fields (v2.6.x Test Feature)

To save memory, you can load only required fields:

# Load only specified fields
client.load_collection(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 load_fields=["book_id", "content_vector"], # Must include primary key and at least one vector field
 skip_load_dynamic_field=True # Skip dynamic fields
)

res = client.get_load_state(collection_name=collection_name)
print(f"📚 Collection '{collection_name}' load status: {res}")

Notes:

  • load_fields must include primary key field
  • load_fields must include at least one vector field
  • Only fields in load_fields can be used as filters and output fields for search and queries

5.4 Releasing Collections 

# Release collection, unload from memory
client.release_collection(collection_name=collection_name)

res = client.get_load_state(collection_name=collection_name)
print(f"📚 Collection '{collection_name}' load status: {res}")

Scenarios for releasing collections:

  • Long-unused collections, release to save memory
  • Need to rebuild index, release first then rebuild
  • System memory insufficient, release unused collections

6. Collection Aliases

6.1 Purpose of Aliases

Aliases allow decoupling client applications from specific physical collection names, enabling seamless updates and blue-green deployments.

Usage scenarios:

  1. Blue-green deployment: Create new collection → Prepare data → Switch alias → Delete old collection
  2. A/B testing: Maintain two collections simultaneously, switch via alias
  3. Version management: prod_data_v1, prod_data_v2, alias always points to latest version

6.2 Key Alias Properties

  • One collection can have multiple aliases
  • One alias can point to only one collection at a time
  • Milvus checks collection name first, then alias if collection not found

6.3 Creating Aliases 

# Create alias for collection
client.create_alias(
 collection_name=collection_name,
 alias="book"
)

# View all aliases for collection
aliases = client.list_aliases(collection_name)
print(f"📚 Collection '{collection_name}' aliases: {aliases}")

6.4 Viewing Alias Details 

# Describe alias
alias_info = client.describe_alias(alias='book')
print(f"📚 Alias 'book' description: {alias_info}")

6.5 Changing Alias Target 

# Create new version collection
if not client.has_collection("book_v2"):
 client.create_collection(
 collection_name="book_v2",
 schema=schemas,
 index_params=index_params
 )

# Point alias to new collection
client.alter_alias(
 alias="book",
 collection_name="book_v2" # New collection name
)

print(f"📚 Alias 'book' now points to 'book_v2'")

6.6 Deleting Aliases 

# Delete alias
client.drop_alias(alias="book")
print(f"📚 Alias 'book' deleted")

6.7 Complete Blue-Green Deployment Example 

def blue_green_deployment():
 """Blue-green deployment example"""

 # 1. Current production uses prod_data_v1
 client.create_alias(
 collection_name="prod_data_v1",
 alias="prod_data"
 )

 # 2. Create new version collection
 client.create_collection(
 collection_name="prod_data_v2",
 schema=schemas,
 index_params=index_params
 )

 # 3. Prepare data in new collection
 # ... insert data, create indexes, test ...

 # 4. Switch alias (zero downtime)
 client.alter_alias(
 alias="prod_data",
 collection_name="prod_data_v2"
 )

 # 5. Application continues using "prod_data" alias without code changes
 # 6. After confirmation, delete old collection
 client.drop_collection("prod_data_v1")

7. Best Practices

7.1 Collection Naming Conventions 

✅ Recommended naming patterns
"user_vectors"
"product_embeddings"
"image_search_collection"
❌ Avoid these naming patterns
"Collection-1" # Avoid hyphens
"集合1" # Avoid non-ASCII characters
"my collection" # Avoid spaces

7.2 Schema Design Recommendations

  1. Primary Key Selection:

    • Use INT64 + auto_id=True for simplicity
    • When using VARCHAR primary keys, ensure uniqueness

  2. Vector Dimensions:

    • Common dimensions: 128, 256, 512, 768, 1024, 1536
    • Higher dimensions mean higher precision but lower performance

  3. Field Count:

    • Avoid too many fields (recommend < 50)
    • Use JSON or STRUCT for complex data

  4. Array Capacity:

    • Set max_capacity reasonably, avoid excessive values
    • The borrow_history setting of 2000 in the example is reasonable

7.3 Index Configuration Recommendations 

# Recommended to use AUTOINDEX, Milvus will automatically select optimal index
index_params.add_index(
field_name="content_vector",
index_type="AUTOINDEX",
metric_type="COSINE"
)
# For large-scale data, manually specify index type
index_params.add_index(
field_name="content_vector",
index_type="HNSW",
metric_type="COSINE",
params={"M": 16, "efConstruction": 200}
)

7.4 Memory Optimization 

# 1. Enable mmap to reduce memory usage
client.alter_collection_properties(
collection_name=collection_name,
properties={"mmap.enabled": True}
)
# 2. Load only necessary fields
client.load_collection(
collection_name=collection_name,
load_fields=["book_id", "content_vector"]
)
# 3. Set reasonable TTL
client.alter_collection_properties(
collection_name=collection_name,
properties={"collection.ttl.seconds": 604800} # 7 days
)

7.5 Performance Optimization 

# 1. Set reasonable shard count
client.create_collection(
collection_name=collection_name,
schema=schemas,
num_shards=4 # Adjust based on concurrent write volume
)
# 2. Choose appropriate consistency level
client.create_collection(
collection_name=collection_name,
schema=schemas,
consistency_level="Session" # Balance performance and consistency
)
# 3. Enable partition key isolation
client.alter_collection_properties(
collection_name=collection_name,
properties={"partitionkey.isolation": True}
)

8. Common Questions

8.1 Why must indexes be specified when creating collections?

Milvus requires at least creating indexes for vector fields when creating collections; otherwise, collections cannot be loaded. If unsure which index to use, recommend using AUTOINDEX.

8.2 How to choose consistency level?

Scenario Recommended Level Reason
Financial transactions Strong Requires strong consistency
Real-time recommendations Bounded Balance performance and consistency
Production environment Session Default recommendation
Big data analysis Eventually Acceptable temporary inconsistency

8.3 When to enable mmap?

  • Enable mmap: Large data volume, limited memory
  • Don’t enable mmap: Small data volume, pursue maximum performance

8.4 How to handle dynamic fields? 

# Enable dynamic fields
client.alter_collection_properties(
collection_name=collection_name,
properties={"dynamicfield.enabled": True}
)
# Can include undefined fields during insertion
client.insert(
collection_name=collection_name,
data=[{
"book_id": 1,
"title": "Python Programming",
"content_vector": [0.1] * 256,
"extra_field": "This is a dynamic field" # Not defined in Schema
}]
)

9. Comparison with MySQL Operations

Operation Milvus MySQL
Create table/collection client.create_collection() CREATE TABLE
View tables/collections client.list_collections() SHOW TABLES
Check existence client.has_collection() SHOW TABLES LIKE 'name'
View structure client.describe_collection() DESCRIBE table_name
Rename client.rename_collection() RENAME TABLE
Delete client.drop_collection() DROP TABLE
Modify properties client.alter_collection_properties() ALTER TABLE

10. Key Points Summary

  1. Schema must be explicit: Must define complete Schema before creating collections
  2. Indexes are mandatory: At least create indexes for vector fields, recommend using AUTOINDEX
  3. Load before querying: Collections need to be loaded into memory before searching
  4. Aliases enable decoupling: Using aliases enables seamless updates and blue-green deployments
  5. mmap saves memory: For large-scale data, enabling mmap significantly reduces memory usage
  6. Consistency level adjustable: Choose appropriate consistency level based on business requirements
  7. TTL auto-cleans: Setting TTL automatically deletes expired data
  8. Sharding improves concurrency: Reasonable shard count improves concurrent write performance

11. References

  • Milvus Official Documentation - Manage Collections
  • Milvus Official Documentation - Schema
  • Milvus Official Documentation - Index

item.title: Milvus Schema Detailed Guide - Field Types and Data Modeling
item.description:
Milvus Schema Learning Document

1. Understanding Schema Concept

1.1 What is Schema?

Schema is the metadata definition of a Collection, similar to table structure definition in relational databases.

Milvus Concept Relational Database Concept Description
Schema Table Schema Metadata defining data structure
Field Column Field/column definition
DataType Data Type Data types (VARCHAR, INT, FLOAT, etc.)
Primary Key Primary Key Primary key field
Vector Field - Milvus-specific vector field
Dynamic Field - Dynamic field, similar to MongoDB’s flexible Schema

1.2 Creating Schema 

from pymilvus import MilvusClient, DataType

# Create Schema
schema = client.create_schema(
 enable_dynamic_field=True # Enable dynamic fields
)

# Compare with MySQL
# CREATE TABLE products (
# ...
# );

2. Field Types Detailed

2.1 Field Classification 

Field Types
├── Scalar Fields
│ ├── String (VARCHAR)
│ ├── Numbers (INT8/INT16/INT32/INT64/FLOAT/DOUBLE/BOOL)
│ ├── JSON
│ ├── Arrays (ARRAY)
│ ├── Timestamp (TIMESTAMPTZ)
│ ├── Struct Arrays (ARRAY of STRUCT)
│ └── Geometry Fields (GEOMETRY)
└── Vector Fields
├── Dense Vectors (FLOAT_VECTOR/FLOAT16_VECTOR/BFLOAT16_VECTOR/INT8_VECTOR)
├── Sparse Vectors (SPARSE_FLOAT_VECTOR)
└── Binary Vectors (BINARY_VECTOR)

3. Primary Key Fields

3.1 Primary Key Type Comparison

Data Type Description Compare with MySQL
INT64 64-bit integer, recommended Similar to BIGINT AUTO_INCREMENT
VARCHAR Variable-length string Similar to VARCHAR(n) PRIMARY KEY

3.2 Auto ID vs Manual ID

Mode Description Use Cases Compare with MySQL
Auto ID Milvus automatically generates unique identifiers Most situations AUTO_INCREMENT
Manual ID User provides unique ID ID needs to be consistent with external systems Manually insert primary key values

3.3 Primary Key Field Examples 

# Auto ID Primary Key
schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_id",
 datatype=DataType.INT64,
 is_primary=True, # Mark as primary key
 auto_id=True # Auto-generate ID
)

# Compare with MySQL
# product_id BIGINT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY

# Manual ID Primary Key (using string)
schema.add_field(
 field_name="sku_code",
 datatype=DataType.VARCHAR,
 max_length=50,
 is_primary=True,
 auto_id=False # Manual ID provision
)

# Compare with MySQL
# sku_code VARCHAR(50) PRIMARY KEY

4. Scalar Fields Detailed

4.1 String Fields (VARCHAR)

Attribute Description Range Compare with MySQL
datatype Data type DataType.VARCHAR VARCHAR
max_length Maximum length 1-65535 n in VARCHAR(n)
nullable Nullable True/False NULL/NOT NULL
default_value Default value String DEFAULT ‘value’ 
# String field example
schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_name",
 datatype=DataType.VARCHAR,
 max_length=255,
 nullable=False,
 default_value="Unnamed Product"
)

# Compare with MySQL
# product_name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'Unnamed Product'

4.2 Numeric Fields

Milvus Type Description Range Compare with MySQL
BOOL Boolean value True/False BOOLEAN
INT8 8-bit integer -128 ~ 127 TINYINT
INT16 16-bit integer -32768 ~ 32767 SMALLINT
INT32 32-bit integer -2^31 ~ 2^31-1 INT
INT64 64-bit integer -2^63 ~ 2^63-1 BIGINT
FLOAT 32-bit float - FLOAT
DOUBLE 64-bit double - DOUBLE 
# Integer field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="stock_quantity",
 datatype=DataType.INT32,
 nullable=False,
 default_value=0
)

# Float field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_price",
 datatype=DataType.FLOAT,
 nullable=False
)

# Boolean field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="is_available",
 datatype=DataType.BOOL,
 default_value=True
)

# Compare with MySQL
# stock_quantity INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
# product_price FLOAT NOT NULL,
# is_available BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE

4.3 JSON Fields

JSON Fields vs Dynamic Fields Comparison

Feature JSON Fields Dynamic Fields Compare with MySQL
Schema Definition Must be explicitly declared Hidden field $meta JSON column vs no equivalent concept
Use Cases Structured, known data Flexible, semi-structured data JSON column vs NoSQL features
Control User controls field names and structure System automatically managed -
Query Method metadata["key"] dynamic_key or $meta["key"] JSON_EXTRACT() 
# JSON field example
schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_detail",
 datatype=DataType.JSON
)

# Insert data example
data = {
 "product_id": 1,
 "product_detail": {
 "brand": "Apple",
 "model": "iPhone 15",
 "specs": {
 "cpu": "A17 Pro",
 "ram": "8GB"
 }
 }
}

# Compare with MySQL
# product_detail JSON
# Query: SELECT JSON_EXTRACT(product_detail, '$.brand') FROM products;

JSON Indexing

JSON indexing suitable scenarios:

  1. Structured Schema with consistent, known keys
  2. Equality and range queries on specific JSON paths
  3. Need for precise control over indexed keys
  4. Efficient storage acceleration for target queries
# Create JSON index
index_params = MilvusClient.prepare_index_params()

index_params.add_index(
 field_name="product_detail", # JSON field name
 index_type="AUTOINDEX", # Must be AUTOINDEX or INVERTED
 index_name="idx_product_brand", # Index name
 params={
 "json_path": 'product_detail["brand"]', # Top-level key
 "json_cast_type": "VARCHAR" # Data type
 }
)

# Nested key indexing
index_params.add_index(
 field_name="product_detail",
 index_type="AUTOINDEX",
 index_name="idx_product_cpu",
 params={
 "json_path": 'product_detail["specs"]["cpu"]', # Nested key
 "json_cast_type": "VARCHAR"
 }
)

# Compare with MySQL 8.0+
# CREATE INDEX idx_product_brand ON products((JSON_EXTRACT(product_detail, '$.brand')));

JSON Shredding Optimization

JSON shredding accelerates queries by converting row-based storage to columnar storage.

Parameter Name Description Default Adjustment Suggestions
common.enabledJSONShredding Whether to enable JSON shredding false Set to true to activate feature
common.usingJSONShreddingForQuery Whether to use shredded data for acceleration true Set to false to restore when queries fail
queryNode.mmap.jsonShredding Whether to use mmap when loading true Adjust for special memory requirements
dataCoord.jsonShreddingMaxColumns Maximum shredded columns 1024 Increase when frequent keys exceed limit
dataCoord.jsonShreddingRatioThreshold Minimum occurrence rate threshold 0.3 Adjust shredding criteria 
# milvus.yaml configuration example
common:
 enabledJSONShredding: true
 usingJSONShreddingForQuery: true

queryNode:
 mmap:
 jsonShredding: true

dataCoord:
 jsonShreddingMaxColumns: 1024
 jsonShreddingRatioThreshold: 0.3

4.4 Array Fields (ARRAY) 

# String array
schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_tags",
 datatype=DataType.ARRAY,
 element_type=DataType.VARCHAR,
 max_capacity=10, # Up to 10 elements
 max_length=50 # Each string up to 50 characters
)

# Integer array
schema.add_field(
 field_name="related_product_ids",
 datatype=DataType.ARRAY,
 element_type=DataType.INT64,
 max_capacity=20
)

# Insert data example
data = {
 "product_id": 1,
 "product_tags": ["Electronics", "Phone", "5G", "Smart"],
 "related_product_ids": [101, 102, 103]
}

# Compare with MySQL 8.0+
# product_tags JSON -- MySQL implements with JSON arrays
# Or use association tables for many-to-many relationships

4.5 Timestamp Fields (TIMESTAMPTZ)

TIMESTAMPTZ is timezone-aware timestamp type.

Feature Description Compare with MySQL
Input Format ISO 8601 strings (with timezone) TIMESTAMP
Internal Storage UTC normalized storage Depends on time_zone setting
Comparison Operations Unified UTC usage May be affected by timezone 
# Timestamp field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="created_at",
 datatype=DataType.TIMESTAMPTZ,
 nullable=False,
 default_value="2025-01-01T00:00:00+00:00"
)

# Insert data example
data = {
 "product_id": 1,
 "created_at": "2025-05-01T23:59:59+08:00" # Beijing time
}

# Compare with MySQL
# created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP

Timezone Management Levels

Level Parameter Scope Priority
Database timezone Default for all collections in database Lowest
Collection timezone Override database default timezone Medium
Query/Search timezone Temporary override for specific operation Highest 
# Database level timezone setting
client.alter_database_properties(
 "my_db",
 {"timezone": "Asia/Shanghai"}
)

# Collection level timezone setting (when creating collection)
client.create_collection(
 collection_name="products",
 schema=schema,
 properties={"timezone": "America/New_York"}
)

# Specify timezone temporarily during query
client.query(
 collection_name="products",
 filter="created_at > '2025-01-01T00:00:00+08:00'",
 timezone="Asia/Shanghai"
)

4.6 Struct Arrays (ARRAY of STRUCT)

Struct arrays are used to store complex nested data.

# Product reviews array example
struct_schema = client.create_struct_field_schema()
struct_schema.add_field(field_name="user_id",datatype=DataType.INT64)
struct_schema.add_field(field_name="rating",datatype=DataType.INT32)
struct_schema.add_field(field_name="comment",datatype=DataType.VARCHAR,max_length=500)
struct_schema.add_field(field_name="review_vector",datatype=DataType.FLOAT_VECTOR,dim=64)
struct_schema.add_field(field_name="timestamp",datatype=DataType.TIMESTAMPTZ)
schema.add_field(
 field_name="reviews",
 datatype=DataType.ARRAY,
 element_type=DataType.STRUCT,
 max_capacity=100,
 element_schema=struct_schema
)

# Insert data example
data = {
 "product_id": 1,
 "reviews": [
 {
 "user_id": 1001,
 "rating": 5,
 "comment": "Very good!",
 "review_vector": [0.1, 0.2, ...], # 64-dimensional vector
 "timestamp": "2025-05-01T10:00:00+08:00"
 },
 {
 "user_id": 1002,
 "rating": 4,
 "comment": "Pretty good",
 "review_vector": [0.3, 0.4, ...],
 "timestamp": "2025-05-02T15:30:00+08:00"
 }
 ]
}

# Compare with MySQL
# Need to create association table reviews
# CREATE TABLE reviews (
# review_id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,
# product_id BIGINT,
# user_id BIGINT,
# rating INT,
# comment VARCHAR(500),
# timestamp TIMESTAMP,
# FOREIGN KEY (product_id) REFERENCES products(product_id)
# );

4.7 Geometry Fields (GEOMETRY)

Used to store geographic location and coordinate information.

# Geometry field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="store_location",
 datatype=DataType.GEOMETRY,
 default_value="POINT(0 0)"
)

# Insert data example
data = {
 "product_id": 1,
 "store_location": "POINT(116.4074 39.9042)" # Tiananmen Square coordinates, Beijing
}

# Compare with MySQL
# store_location POINT NOT NULL
# Or use PostGIS extension GEOMETRY type

5. Vector Fields Detailed

5.1 Vector Type Comparison

Milvus Type Description Use Cases Storage Size
FLOAT_VECTOR 32-bit float vector High-precision semantic search 4 bytes/dim
FLOAT16_VECTOR 16-bit half-precision float Deep learning, GPU computing 2 bytes/dim
BFLOAT16_VECTOR 16-bit brain float Large-scale image retrieval 2 bytes/dim
INT8_VECTOR 8-bit integer vector Quantized models, fast inference 1 byte/dim
SPARSE_FLOAT_VECTOR Sparse float vector Full-text search, term matching Variable
BINARY_VECTOR Binary vector Resource-constrained scenarios 1 bit/dim

5.2 Dense Vector Examples 

# FLOAT_VECTOR - Standard dense vector
schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_embedding",
 datatype=DataType.FLOAT_VECTOR,
 dim=128 # 128-dimensional vector
)

# FLOAT16_VECTOR - Half-precision vector
schema.add_field(
 field_name="image_embedding",
 datatype=DataType.FLOAT16_VECTOR,
 dim=512
)

# INT8_VECTOR - Quantized vector
schema.add_field(
 field_name="text_embedding_quantized",
 datatype=DataType.INT8_VECTOR,
 dim=256
)

# Insert data example
import numpy as np

data = {
 "product_id": 1,
 "product_embedding": np.random.rand(128).tolist(), # 128-dim float vector
 "image_embedding": np.random.rand(512).astype(np.float16).tolist(),
 "text_embedding_quantized": np.random.randint(-128, 127, 256).tolist()
}

# MySQL has no equivalent type, needs to be stored as BLOB or JSON

5.3 Sparse Vector Examples 

# Sparse vector field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="keyword_vector",
 datatype=DataType.SPARSE_FLOAT_VECTOR
)

# Insert data example (sparse vector format)
data = {
 "product_id": 1,
 "keyword_vector": {
 10: 0.5, # Index 10 value is 0.5
 25: 0.8, # Index 25 value is 0.8
 100: 0.3 # Index 100 value is 0.3
 }
}

6. Dynamic Fields

6.1 Dynamic Field Concept

Dynamic fields allow inserting fields not defined in Schema, which are automatically stored in the hidden $meta JSON field.

# Enable dynamic fields
schema = client.create_schema(
 enable_dynamic_field=True # Enable dynamic fields
)

# Define basic fields
schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_id",
 datatype=DataType.INT64,
 is_primary=True,
 auto_id=True
)

schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_vector",
 datatype=DataType.FLOAT_VECTOR,
 dim=128
)

# Can include undefined fields when inserting data
data = {
 "product_id": 1,
 "product_vector": [0.1, 0.2, ...],
 "name": "iPhone 15", # Not defined in Schema
 "category": "Electronics", # Not defined in Schema
 "price": 999.99 # Not defined in Schema
}

# Milvus internally stores as:
# {
# "product_id": 1,
# "product_vector": [0.1, 0.2, ...],
# "$meta": {
# "name": "iPhone 15",
# "category": "Electronics",
# "price": 999.99
# }
# }

6.2 Supported Types for Dynamic Fields

Type Milvus DataType Compare with MongoDB
String VARCHAR String
Integer INT8, INT32, INT64 Int32, Int64
Float FLOAT, DOUBLE Double
Boolean BOOL Boolean
Array ARRAY Array
JSON Object JSON Object

6.3 Querying Dynamic Fields 

# Direct field name query
results = client.query(
 collection_name="products",
 filter='name == "iPhone 15"', # Directly use dynamic field name
 output_fields=["product_id", "name", "category", "price"]
)

# Or query through $meta
results = client.query(
 collection_name="products",
 filter='$meta["category"] == "Electronics"',
 output_fields=["product_id", "$meta"]
)

# Compare with MongoDB
# db.products.find({"name": "iPhone 15"})

7. Complete Schema Example 

from pymilvus import MilvusClient, DataType

# Connect client
client = MilvusClient(uri="http://localhost:19530", token="root:Milvus")

# Create Schema
schema = client.create_schema(
 enable_dynamic_field=True # Enable dynamic fields
)

# 1. Primary key field (Auto ID)
schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_id",
 datatype=DataType.INT64,
 is_primary=True,
 auto_id=True
)

# 2. String field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_name",
 datatype=DataType.VARCHAR,
 max_length=255
)

# 3. Numeric field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_price",
 datatype=DataType.FLOAT
)

# 4. JSON field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_detail",
 datatype=DataType.JSON
)

# 5. Array field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_tags",
 datatype=DataType.ARRAY,
 element_type=DataType.VARCHAR,
 max_capacity=10,
 max_length=50
)

# 6. Timestamp field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="created_at",
 datatype=DataType.TIMESTAMPTZ
)

# 7. Struct array
struct_schema = client.create_struct_field_schema()
struct_schema.add_field(field_name="user_id",datatype=DataType.INT64)
struct_schema.add_field(field_name="rating",datatype=DataType.INT32)
struct_schema.add_field(field_name="comment",datatype=DataType.VARCHAR,max_length=500)
struct_schema.add_field(field_name="review_vector",datatype=DataType.FLOAT_VECTOR,dim=64)
struct_schema.add_field(field_name="timestamp",datatype=DataType.TIMESTAMPTZ)
schema.add_field(
 field_name="reviews",
 datatype=DataType.ARRAY,
 element_type=DataType.STRUCT,
 max_capacity=100,
 element_schema=struct_schema
)

# 8. Geometry field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="store_location",
 datatype=DataType.GEOMETRY,
 default_value="POINT(0 0)"
)

# 9. Dense vector field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="product_embedding",
 datatype=DataType.FLOAT_VECTOR,
 dim=128
)

# 10. Sparse vector field
schema.add_field(
 field_name="keyword_vector",
 datatype=DataType.SPARSE_FLOAT_VECTOR
)

print("✅ Schema creation completed")

8. Schema vs MySQL Comparison Summary

Operation Milvus MySQL
Create Schema client.create_schema() CREATE TABLE
Add Field schema.add_field() ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN
Primary Key Definition is_primary=True PRIMARY KEY
Auto ID auto_id=True AUTO_INCREMENT
Nullable Field nullable=True NULL
Default Value default_value=value DEFAULT value
Vector Fields FLOAT_VECTOR etc. No equivalent type
Dynamic Fields enable_dynamic_field=True None (need JSON or NoSQL)

9. Best Practices

9.1 Field Naming Conventions 

# ✅ Recommended
"product_id"
"user_name"
"created_at"

# ❌ Avoid
"Product-ID" # Avoid hyphens
"用户名" # Avoid non-ASCII
"field name" # Avoid spaces

9.2 Vector Dimension Selection

Scenario Recommended Dimensions Model Examples
Text Embedding 384-768 sentence-transformers
Image Embedding 512-2048 ResNet, CLIP
Multimodal 512-1024 CLIP, ALIGN

9.3 Index Strategy 

# Create index for vector fields
index_params = MilvusClient.prepare_index_params()

# HNSW index (recommended for high-precision search)
index_params.add_index(
 field_name="product_embedding",
 index_type="HNSW",
 metric_type="COSINE",
 params={"M": 16, "efConstruction": 256}
)

# IVF_FLAT index (suitable for large-scale data)
index_params.add_index(
 field_name="image_embedding",
 index_type="IVF_FLAT",
 metric_type="L2",
 params={"nlist": 1024}
)

10. Key Points Summary

  1. Schema Flexibility: Milvus supports dynamic fields, more flexible than traditional relational databases
  2. Native Vector Support: Vector fields are first-class citizens, no extra conversion needed
  3. Rich Types: Supports JSON, arrays, structs and other complex types
  4. Timezone Awareness: TIMESTAMPTZ provides native timezone support
  5. Index Optimization: JSON shredding, vector indexing and other optimization techniques
  6. NoSQL Integration: Dynamic fields similar to MongoDB, but retain Schema constraint advantages

item.title: Milvus Database Management - To Be Completed
item.description:
Milvus Database Management Guide

Note: This document is currently under development and will be updated with complete content soon.

Coming Soon

This guide will cover comprehensive Milvus database management topics including:

  • Database creation and configuration
  • Collection management
  • Performance optimization
  • Backup and recovery
  • Monitoring and maintenance
  • Security and access control
  • Cluster management
  • Resource allocation and scaling

Planned Content Structure

1. Database Fundamentals

  • Understanding Milvus architecture
  • Database vs Collection concepts
  • Storage engines overview

2. Administration Tasks

  • Creating and managing databases
  • User and permission management
  • Configuration tuning
  • Health monitoring

3. Performance Optimization

  • Index selection strategies
  • Query optimization techniques
  • Resource allocation best practices
  • Troubleshooting common issues

4. Production Operations

  • Backup and disaster recovery
  • Scaling strategies
  • Monitoring and alerting
  • Maintenance procedures

Stay tuned for updates!

item.title: Complete Guide to Installing Milvus Standalone Mode and Common Problem Solutions
item.description:
Introduction

Milvus is an open-source vector database designed specifically for handling large-scale vector data, supporting vector similarity search, real-time queries, and other features. The Standalone mode is Milvus’s single-machine embedded deployment method, suitable for learning, testing, and small-scale applications. It runs using Docker containers with embedded etcd as metadata storage, requiring no external dependencies.

During the actual installation process, I encountered multiple issues including non-existent image tags, network connection refusals, container permission issues causing startup failures (Exited 134), and scripts getting stuck in waiting states. This article is based on real installation experience on a VMware Ubuntu virtual machine on February 24, 2026, providing complete steps and systematically summarizing problems and solutions. The host machine configuration is Intel Core i9-12900H + 16 GB RAM.

Environment Preparation

System Requirements

  • Ubuntu 20.04 or higher version (22.04 LTS recommended)
  • Docker 20.10 or higher version
  • Minimum VM configuration recommendation: 8-12 GB RAM, 4-8 vCPU (recommended 1 processor × 6-8 cores)
  • Network: Domestic mirror accelerator recommended

Configure Docker Image Accelerator

Edit /etc/docker/daemon.json:

{
 "registry-mirrors": [
 "https://docker.xuanyuan.me",
 "https://mirror.ccs.tencentyun.com",
 "https://hub-mirror.c.163.com",
 "https://docker.mirrors.ustc.edu.cn"
 ]
}

Restart Docker:

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl restart docker

Verify accelerator configuration:

sudo docker info | grep -A 10 "Registry Mirrors"

Installation Steps

1. Download Official Standalone Embedded Startup Script 

wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/milvus-io/milvus/master/scripts/standalone_embed.sh
chmod +x standalone_embed.sh

2. Modify Image Tag (v2.6.11 Not Pushed, Use Latest Available Build from Master Branch)

Edit standalone_embed.sh, find the image line in the docker run command, change:

milvusdb/milvus:v2.6.11 \

to:

milvusdb/milvus:master-20260224-091b147e-amd64 \

or a more stable previous day version:

milvusdb/milvus:master-20260223-49bf0b63-amd64 \

Save the file.

3. Create and Set Data Directory Permissions (Most Critical Step, Avoid Permission Denial) 

# Delete old directory (prevent residual permission errors)
rm -rf ./volumes/milvus

# Create new directory
mkdir -p ./volumes/milvus

# Set owner and permissions (ensure non-root user in container can write)
sudo chown -R $USER:$USER ./volumes/milvus
chmod -R 775 ./volumes/milvus

# Verify permissions (should show drwxrwxr-x ai-stu ai-stu)
ls -ld ./volumes/milvus

4. Start Milvus 

bash standalone_embed.sh start

5. Verify Success

Wait for script output “Start successfully.”

Check container status:

sudo docker ps

Should show Up ... (healthy)

Test health endpoint:

curl http://localhost:19530/healthz

Should return "ok"

Common Problems Summary and Solutions

Problem 1: Image Pull Failure (not found / connection refused)

Phenomenon:

  • Unable to find image ... not found or dial tcp ... connection refused

Cause:

  • v2.6.11 tag not yet pushed to Docker Hub
  • Domestic network access to registry-1.docker.io is restricted

Solutions:

  1. Use stable version tags: Switch to released stable versions like v2.6.10 
    sudo docker run -d \
 --name milvus-standalone \
 --gpus all \
 -e ETCD_USE_EMBED=true \
 -e COMMON_STORAGETYPE=local \
 -p 19530:19530 \
 -v ${DOCKER_VOLUME_DIRECTORY:-.}/volumes/milvus:/var/lib/milvus \
 milvusdb/milvus:v2.6.10
  2. Configure domestic accelerators (Xuanyuan, Alibaba Cloud, NetEase, etc.)
  3. Offline transfer method: pull + save on accessible network machine:
docker pull milvusdb/milvus:master-20260224-091b147e-amd64
docker save -o milvus.tar milvusdb/milvus:master-20260224-091b147e-amd64
gzip milvus.tar

Load after transferring to server:

gunzip milvus.tar.gz
sudo docker load -i milvus.tar

Problem 2: Container Exits Immediately After Startup (Exited 134)

Phenomenon:

  • sudo docker ps -a shows Exited (134)

Cause:

  • SIGABRT signal, internal process abort
  • Usually caused by permission denial, initialization failure, or insufficient memory

Solutions:

  1. Check logs: 
    sudo docker logs milvus-standalone --tail 100
  2. Increase VM memory to 8-12 GB
  3. Clean and reset permissions (see step 3)
  4. Try more stable tags (such as master-20260223-49bf0b63-amd64)

Problem 3: Permission Denied (mkdir /var/lib/milvus/data/: permission denied)

Phenomenon:

  • Logs show [FATAL] ... permission denied and panic

Cause:

  • Host ./volumes/milvus directory lacks sufficient permissions
  • Non-root user in container cannot create subdirectories

Solutions:

  1. Must delete old directory first: 
    rm -rf ./volumes/milvus
  2. Recreate and set permissions (step 3)
  3. Recommend using 775 permissions to ensure group writability

Problem 4: Script Stuck at “Wait for Milvus Starting…”

Phenomenon:

  • Script shows no output for extended period

Cause:

  • Container hasn’t reached healthy state (crashed or health check failed)

Solutions:

  1. Check status in new terminal: 
    sudo docker ps -a
  2. View real-time logs: 
    sudo docker logs -f milvus-standalone
  3. Manually test health endpoint: 
    curl http://localhost:19530/healthz
  4. Clean data volumes and retry
